Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe sit down when I felt like it, put on some lecture recordings or podcasts related to the course, and just listen while doing other stuff. Sometimes I'd get into it for a few hours, other times I'd go days without touching anything. It was pretty random and depended on my mood more than any actual plan.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd cram when deadlines were close, but otherwise I'd drift through the weeks without much structure. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks almost nothing. It really depended on what else was going on and whether I could be bothered to focus on coursework.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or an email notification would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if anything new was posted, scroll around for a bit, and then close it without doing much. Boredom sometimes pushed me to log in too, but that didn't mean I actually engaged with anything meaningful once I was there.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd use it when I had to, but it wasn't something I naturally gravitated toward. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of interesting at first, but even that novelty wore off pretty quick. It just never clicked as part of my daily routine.
Q5: The video lectures and audio recordings were the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could put them on while lying in bed or walking around, which felt less like studying. The discussion forums were okay too because I could skim through what others were saying and pick up on concepts I might have missed.
Q6: The reading materials and written assignments I pretty much ignored unless absolutely necessary. Reading long documents on screen bores me to death. I'd download stuff with good intentions but rarely actually read it. The quizzes and practice exercises also got skipped a lot because they felt tedious and I wasn't motivated enough to bother.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was quick, had some interactive element, or if I could see an immediate payoff. The gamified tasks were decent because at least there was some sense of progress or achievement. But if something felt like it would take forever with no clear benefit, I'd just skip it without thinking twice.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely ignorable to me. If it wasn't graded or required, I probably wasn't doing it. Required stuff at least had consequences, so I'd force myself eventually. But optional? That was basically code for 'you can skip this' in my mind, even if it might have been helpful.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone away from the computer. Sometimes I'd have conversations with classmates about course content, or I'd look up YouTube videos explaining concepts in a more engaging way. A lot of my actual learning happened through audio content that wasn't tracked anywhere.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was listening to downloaded lectures while commuting or doing chores. I'd also talk through concepts with friends over the phone, which helped me understand things better. None of that showed up in the LMS. My engagement looked way lower than it actually was because the system couldn't see those activities.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or audio file once, and then I'd replay it multiple times offline. The LMS would only register that single download, not the fact that I listened to the same lecture three or four times while trying to absorb the material. It was more convenient to study offline anyway.
Q12: Around midterms everything felt overwhelming and pointless at the same time. I was stressed but also just bored with the material. When assignments piled up and I fell behind, it became harder to care. The less I engaged, the more disconnected I felt, and that made it even harder to jump back in.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. I'd be tired and just not have the mental energy to focus on course stuff. Competing responsibilities like social stuff or just wanting to relax would win out over logging into the LMS. When I was confused about something, I'd often just give up rather than seek help because it felt like too much effort.
Q14: Yeah, plenty of times I'd open up materials and just zone out completely. I'd have a video playing but not actually paying attention, or I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere even though technically I was 'studying.' It was more about going through the motions than actually learning.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd keep putting them off because they felt overwhelming or boring. I'd tell myself I'd do them later, and then later would become the night before the deadline. It's not that I didn't know better; I just couldn't make myself care enough to start earlier.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit anything. I'd ignore the LMS for weeks and then frantically log in when something was due. My usage would spike right before deadlines and then drop to almost nothing afterward. Without that pressure, I probably wouldn't have done much at all.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. If I got good feedback, I'd feel briefly satisfied but it wouldn't make me more engaged going forward. Bad feedback just made me feel like the effort wasn't worth it anyway. I know feedback is supposed to be motivating, but for me it didn't really move the needle.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty loose, which in theory should have been good for me, but it actually made it easier to drift. Without strict schedules forcing me to engage, I'd just float along and do the minimum. More structure might have helped push me to stay on track, but I also might have resented it.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement slightly easier because there was something to work toward. But the heavy reliance on reading and written assignments made it harder for someone like me who learns by listening. If there had been more audio content or interactive discussions, I probably would have engaged more.
Q20: I think some students might be exploring the platform, looking at different materials, trying to figure out what's going on, but then struggling to actually complete the work. Or they might be dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to follow through. High activity doesn't mean productive activity; sometimes you're just clicking around aimlessly.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. I didn't log in much or do a lot of visible activity, but I still managed to pass with Merit because I'd absorb enough through listening to recordings and occasional cramming. The LMS doesn't capture everything, so someone can look disengaged but still be learning in their own way outside the system.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss offline study, conversations with peers, and the mental effort you put in. They're like counting steps without knowing if you actually went anywhere meaningful.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort because you want to understand, not just because you have to. For me, I rarely felt that this semester. I was going through the motions most of the time. Real engagement would mean being curious and invested, which I wasn't.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I was bored out of my mind most of the time, or that I was dealing with stress that made it hard to focus. It wouldn't capture the audio lectures I listened to repeatedly offline, or the conversations I had with friends about course content. My actual experience was way more complicated than what the data could show.
